metadata.title: Why an Invisible Change Can Flip a Million-Pixel Answer
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism candidate 05. Source chapter: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/08-robustness-what-understanding-means-when-a-pixel-can-break-the-model.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty blue = the human-visible truth (panda), terracotta = the perturbation/attack, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty blue #5B7B9C = the human-visible truth / panda side; terracotta #D35F43 = the perturbation and the flipped label; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Facts verified 2026-07-06 (FACTCHECK.md): Goodfellow, Shlens & Szegedy, 'Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples' (arXiv Dec 2014 / ICLR 2015) — panda 57.7% -> gibbon 99.3% at epsilon 0.007 (closes the chapter's own [Verify] tag). Mechanism: the linearity hypothesis, formula kept OFF screen (delta-f = eps*||w||_1 lives in FACTCHECK only). Card exclusions: no FGSM/PGD equations beyond sign-of-gradient intuition, no L-inf/L1 formalism, no boundary-tilting second account, no defense toolkit. Title's 'million-pixel' is evocative; narration says 'hundreds of thousands of numbers' (224x224x3 = 150,528).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B11: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. No pixel changed enough for you to see. And the model's answer flipped — with more confidence than it had before.

card.copy: Why an invisible change can flip a million-pixel answer

beats.narration_text: This is a panda. You can see that. An image classifier looks and agrees — 'panda,' fifty-seven point seven percent confident.
beats.scene_description: a photograph of a giant panda in bamboo, printed and pinned to the newsprint ground, slow push-in; a small blue chip in the corner: 'panda · 57.7%'
beats.image_prompt: photograph of a giant panda sitting in bamboo, desaturated print reproduction pinned to aged newsprint, editorial collage

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B02.png (or .mp4)

beats.narration_text: Now the second picture. To your eye it is the same picture — same bamboo, same pose, same panda. Pixel by pixel, the differences are fractions of a percent.
beats.scene_description: the same panda plate duplicated side by side; a charcoal serif label between them: 'spot the difference' — no ring, no X

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B03.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: [PLATE+LABEL] identical twins — spot the difference

beats.narration_text: The classifier looks at the second picture and says 'gibbon.' Not hesitantly — ninety-nine point three percent confident. More sure of the gibbon than it ever was of the panda.

document.attribution: — the model, on the same panda (Goodfellow et al., 2014)

beats.scene_description: the verdict set large in charcoal serif (short single line — highlighter-safe); gold sweeps 'gibbon' as it is spoken

beats.narration_text: To see how, forget pictures. To the model, the image is a long list of numbers — hundreds of thousands of them — each one a pixel's brightness.

production_viz.label: the image, as the model sees it
production_viz.mechanic: a small grey pixel-grid draws in at left; an arrow unfurls it into a horizontal strip of number cells (mono digits) — the picture becomes a list
production_viz.note: cell values are dressing; 'hundreds of thousands' is the honest count (224x224x3 = 150,528)

beats.new_visual_element: [DIAGRAM] the pixel grid unfurls into numbers

beats.narration_text: The model multiplies each number by a weight — how hard that pixel pushes toward 'panda' or away from it — and adds everything up. One enormous weighted sum.
beats.scene_description: weight chips stamp beneath each number cell; a vertical activation bar appears at right with a hairline decision line across it, labeled clear of the tube

production_viz.mechanic: small slate weight chips land under the cells; the activation bar and its decision line draw in; the bar sits at a modest 'panda-side' level in blue
production_viz.note: no formula anywhere — the machine is drawn, not written (card exclusion)

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] weights stamp in; the sum bar appears

beats.narration_text: Now nudge every pixel by a sliver — far too small to see — but each sliver in exactly the direction that pixel's weight prefers. The pushes never cancel. They only add.

production_viz.label: a sliver per pixel, all aligned
production_viz.mechanic: tiny terracotta arrows land on every number cell in an accumulating wave — every arrow pointing the same way — while the activation bar climbs in steps, turning terracotta as it rises
production_viz.note: THE core image — sign-of-the-gradient as aligned slivers; the accumulation is the argument

viz.label: slivers accumulate
viz.note: one arrow per visible cell; the bar carries the sum

beats.narration_text: A hundred thousand invisible slivers, all pulling the same way, add up to a shove. The sum crosses the decision line — and the label flips.
beats.scene_description: the bar's fill crosses the decision line; the label chip transforms from blue 'panda' to terracotta 'gibbon'; the editor's pen rings the crossing point — the film's single ring, timed to 'flips'

production_viz.mechanic: fill crosses the hairline; chip Transform panda -> gibbon; HandRing on the crossing
production_viz.note: one ring only — the film's single ring

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the sum crosses; panda becomes gibbon

beats.narration_text: That is the trick of high dimensions. Per pixel, nothing happened. Across hundreds of thousands of pixels, everything did. Invisible in every coordinate — overwhelming in the sum.

production_viz.label: per pixel: nothing · in the sum: everything
production_viz.mechanic: two panels: left, one number cell magnified with its single tiny arrow, labeled 'one pixel — nothing'; right, the full climbed bar, labeled 'the sum — everything'
production_viz.note: the two-scales lesson, drawn as a compare

beats.narration_text: And here's the honest reading: the picture didn't change — what the model was actually using did. The perturbation isn't noise. It's a flashlight, pointed at what the model really learned.
beats.scene_description: the reading set large (short single line — highlighter-safe); gold sweeps 'flashlight'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [QUOTE] not a bug — a flashlight

beats.narration_text: A model can be right for reasons that are not your reasons. The invisible flip is how you find out — before the world finds out for you.

card.copy: Right answer, wrong reasons — until someone checks.
card.sub: from Computational Skepticism for AI — chapter 8

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — right answer, wrong reasons
